sections.heading: What is OOP and why does it exist?
sections.body:
OOP is a way of organising code around OBJECTS — bundles of data (fields) and behaviour (methods) that work together.

Before OOP, programs were long lists of instructions (procedural). When they grew large, they became unmaintainable — any change could break anything else.

OOP solves this by:
  1. Grouping related data and behaviour together (Encapsulation)
  2. Sharing common structure without copy-pasting (Inheritance)
  3. Letting different objects respond to the same message differently (Polymorphism)
  4. Hiding internal complexity (Abstraction)

These four pillars are not just vocabulary — they are design tools. Each one solves a specific class of problems.

sections.heading: Pillar 1 — Encapsulation: hide the internals
Pillar 2 — Abstraction: show what, hide how
Pillar 3 — Inheritance: share structure
Pillar 4 — Polymorphism: one interface, many forms

title: S — Single Responsibility
tagline: One class, one reason to change

sections.heading: The core idea
sections.body:
"A class should have only one reason to change."
— Robert C. Martin

This does NOT mean "a class can only have one method."
It means: if you need to change this class, there should be only ONE source of that change.

WHY does this matter?
  → When a class has multiple responsibilities, changing one responsibility risks breaking others.
  → Small, focused classes are easier to test — you test one thing at a time.
  → Small classes are easier to name — if you struggle to name a class, it probably does too much.

Ask yourself: "If the business changes X, do I need to change this class? What about if they change Y?"
If the answer involves multiple different reasons → split the class.

sections.heading: Beginner example — the obvious violation
The fix — split into focused classes
Advanced — SRP in Spring Boot controllers

title: O — Open/Closed
tagline: Open for extension, closed for modification

sections.heading: The core idea
sections.body:
"Software entities should be open for extension, but closed for modification."
— Bertrand Meyer

OPEN for extension = you can add new behaviour.
CLOSED for modification = you don't edit existing, tested code to do it.

The pain it prevents:
  Imagine you have an OrderProcessor that handles payment. You need to add PayPal support.
  
  BAD approach: edit OrderProcessor, add another if/else block for PayPal.
  → You touched working code. You might break Stripe. You re-test everything.
  
  GOOD approach: define a PaymentStrategy interface, add PayPalStrategy class.
  → Zero existing code changed. Zero regression risk. OrderProcessor is CLOSED to modification.

The key tool: interfaces + polymorphism.
You close a class by making it depend on abstractions (interfaces).
You extend it by adding new implementations of those abstractions.

sections.heading: Beginner — the if/else trap
Intermediate — Strategy Pattern (OCP in action)
Advanced — Spring Boot notification system

title: L — Liskov Substitution
tagline: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types

sections.heading: The core idea
sections.body:
"If S is a subtype of T, then objects of type T may be replaced with objects of type S without altering the correctness of the program."
— Barbara Liskov (1987)

In plain English: if you have code that works with a base class, and you swap it for a subclass, the code must still work correctly — no surprises, no exceptions, no silent failures.

This is the hardest principle to understand, but the simplest to remember via the pain it causes:

VIOLATION: Square extends Rectangle.
  A Rectangle can have width ≠ height.
  A Square CANNOT (both must be equal).
  If code sets rectangle.setWidth(5) and then rectangle.setHeight(10) expecting area=50,
  and a Square is passed in, the area is 100 (both dimensions became 10). 
  The contract is broken.

RULE OF THUMB: if your subclass OVERRIDES a method to throw an exception or do nothing,
you're almost certainly violating LSP.

sections.heading: The classic Square/Rectangle violation
Real Spring Boot LSP violation — throwing unexpected exceptions
LSP and Preconditions / Postconditions

title: I — Interface Segregation
tagline: Don't force classes to implement what they don't need

sections.heading: The core idea
sections.body:
"Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use."
— Robert C. Martin

The pain it prevents:
  You define a big "do everything" interface.
  A class that only needs 20% of it must still implement the other 80%.
  The other 80% either throws UnsupportedOperationException or does nothing.
  Now every change to that 80% forces a recompile of the class that never used it.

The fix: make your interfaces small and focused — each covers one cohesive set of behaviour.
Clients depend only on the interfaces whose methods they actually use.

Corollary: many small interfaces > one big fat interface.

Spring Boot example: UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long>
JpaRepository gives you 20+ methods. ISP says: if you only use findById and save,
your service should depend on a narrow interface, not the entire JpaRepository.

sections.heading: The fat interface — beginner example
Spring Boot — Repository ISP
Advanced — Controller ISP with use-case interfaces

title: D — Dependency Inversion
tagline: Depend on abstractions, not on concretions

sections.heading: The core idea
sections.body:
"High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions."
— Robert C. Martin

Two rules:
  1. High-level modules (business logic) should not import or instantiate low-level modules (DB, email, HTTP).
  2. Abstractions should not depend on details. Details should depend on abstractions.

The word "Inversion" is key — it flips the dependency direction.

Without DIP:
  OrderService (high-level) → creates → MySQLOrderRepository (low-level)
  OrderService is now GLUED to MySQL. Test it? You need MySQL running.
  Switch to PostgreSQL? Edit OrderService.

With DIP:
  OrderService (high-level) → depends on → OrderRepository (interface/abstraction)
  MySQLOrderRepository → implements → OrderRepository
  
  Now OrderService doesn't know or care about MySQL.
  Swap the implementation? OrderService: zero changes.
  Write tests? Inject a MockOrderRepository.

THIS is what Spring's Dependency Injection (DI) container does for you — it manages the wiring.

sections.heading: Beginner — the hardcoded dependency
Spring Boot DI — constructor injection done right
Advanced — DIP enables testability

title: Composition over Inheritance
tagline: HAS-A beats IS-A — build by combining, not by extending

sections.heading: The core idea
sections.body:
"Favour object composition over class inheritance."
— Gang of Four (Design Patterns, 1994)

Inheritance creates a tight, compile-time coupling between parent and child.
Composition creates a loose, runtime coupling between collaborating objects.

INHERITANCE (IS-A):
  Dog extends Animal. Dog IS-AN Animal.
  Dog automatically has all of Animal's methods.
  Problem: you can't change Dog's behaviour at runtime.
  Problem: if Animal changes, Dog is automatically affected.
  Problem: what if you want a behaviour from multiple "parents"? Java has no multiple inheritance.

COMPOSITION (HAS-A):
  Dog HAS-A MovementStrategy (run, swim, fly).
  Dog HAS-A SoundStrategy (bark, whimper, growl).
  Dog HAS-A EatingBehaviour.
  You can change any of these at runtime — give the dog a new MovementStrategy.
  Each behaviour is tested independently.
  Behaviours can be reused across unrelated classes.

The rule: use inheritance ONLY when a true IS-A relationship exists AND the hierarchy is shallow.
Everything else: use composition.

sections.heading: The inheritance explosion problem
The Decorator Pattern — composing behaviour dynamically
Spring Boot composition — service building blocks
When IS inheritance actually correct?
